title: Cybersecurity Basics
description: Learn essential cybersecurity fundamentals including password security, phishing recognition, and social engineering defense strategies.

nodes.title: Password Security Fundamentals
nodes.content: Passwords are the first line of defense in cybersecurity. A strong password uses a combination of uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters with a minimum length of 12 characters. Never reuse passwords across multiple accounts—if one service is breached, attackers gain access to all your accounts. Password managers securely store and manage complex passwords without requiring memorization. Changing passwords regularly further reduces the risk of unauthorized access.

nodes.title: Password Strength Examples
nodes.content: Compare 'Summer2024!' versus 'K9!mPqR2@xL8n'. The first uses a predictable pattern (season + year + punctuation) that attackers can guess. The second randomly mixes character types, making it exponentially harder to crack. A 12-character random password has approximately 95^12 possible combinations—roughly 475 quadrillion possibilities. Even with a computer checking one billion passwords per second, this would require over 15 million years to crack, far exceeding any reasonable threat timeline.

nodes.title: Password Security Assessment
nodes.content: Test your understanding of password security principles and best practices.

questions.question: What is the recommended minimum length for a strong password?
options: 6 characters
8 characters
12 characters
20 characters
feedback: Too short; vulnerable to brute force attacks
Still too short for modern security standards
Correct! 12 characters is the recommended minimum
While secure, 12 characters is the standard recommendation
questions.explanation: Modern security standards recommend a minimum of 12 characters to effectively resist brute force attacks and provide adequate entropy against unauthorized access attempts.

questions.question: Which password is the strongest?
options: Summer2024
LetMeIn123
Tr0p!cal#Sun9Kx@
Secure456
feedback: Uses a predictable pattern of season and year
Contains a predictable English phrase pattern
Correct! Uses random mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters
Too short and uses a common word pattern
questions.explanation: Strong passwords combine uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters in unpredictable ways that cannot be derived from personal information or common language patterns.

nodes.title: Phishing & Social Engineering Attacks
nodes.content: Phishing attacks deceive users into revealing sensitive information by impersonating trusted entities like banks or employers. Social engineering manipulates people through psychological tactics rather than technical exploits. These attacks arrive via email, phone, SMS, or messaging apps and create artificial urgency or appeal to authority. Legitimate organizations never request passwords, credit card numbers, or personal information via unsolicited communications. The strongest defense is verifying requests independently through official channels before taking action.

nodes.title: Analyzing a Phishing Email
nodes.content: You receive an email from 'no-reply@banking-secure.com' claiming 'Your account has been locked. Click here immediately to verify your identity.' Red flags: the sender domain mimics but doesn't match your actual bank, artificial urgency pressures you to act without thinking, and legitimate banks never request password verification via email. The correct response is to contact your bank directly using the phone number on your debit card or visit their official website to check your account status independently.

nodes.title: Phishing & Social Engineering Assessment
nodes.content: Evaluate your ability to identify phishing attacks and social engineering tactics.

questions.question: What is a primary red flag of a phishing email?
options: It arrives during business hours
It creates artificial urgency and requests personal information
It uses company letterhead and logo
It is addressed to you by name
feedback: Phishing attacks arrive at any time of day
Correct! Real sense of urgency combined with requests for sensitive data are classic phishing hallmarks
Attackers routinely replicate official designs and branding
Using your name is actually a common personalization tactic in phishing
questions.explanation: Legitimate companies never create artificial time pressure demanding immediate action, and they never request passwords, account numbers, or personal information through unsolicited email.

questions.question: What is the safest action when you receive a suspicious email claiming to be from your bank?
options: Click the link to verify your account quickly
Reply to the email with your information for verification
Call your bank using the number on your debit card or visit their official website
Forward it to colleagues to see if they received the same email
feedback: This is exactly what phishers want you to do!
Never reply to suspicious communications with sensitive information
Correct! Always verify through officially known contact information independently
Forwarding spreads the phishing attempt and doesn't verify authenticity
questions.explanation: Always contact organizations directly using official contact information that you find yourself, never through links or numbers provided in suspicious communications of unknown origin.

nodes.title: Cybersecurity Practice & Reinforcement
nodes.content: Reinforce your learning with additional practice questions covering password management, authentication, and phishing defense.

questions.question: What does MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) add to password security?
options: A requirement to use longer passwords
A second verification method like a code or fingerprint
Automatic password changes every month
Better tracking of login locations
feedback: This is a password policy change, not MFA
Correct! MFA requires multiple independent verification methods to gain access
MFA does not automatically change passwords on a schedule
MFA doesn't inherently track locations, though some systems do
questions.explanation: Multi-Factor Authentication requires at least two different verification methods, dramatically improving security because an attacker would need to compromise multiple systems or devices simultaneously.

questions.question: Which is the best practice for managing multiple account passwords?
options: Use the same strong password on all accounts
Write passwords in a notebook kept in a safe location
Use a password manager with a strong master password
Use simple passwords that you can easily remember
feedback: If one account is breached, all your accounts are immediately compromised
Physical notebooks can be lost, stolen, or discovered
Correct! Password managers encrypt and securely store unique passwords for each account
Simple, memorable passwords are easily guessed and vulnerable to brute force attacks
questions.explanation: A dedicated password manager is the most secure method because it allows you to maintain unique, complex passwords for each account without memorization or physical storage risks.

nodes.title: Next Steps in Your Cybersecurity Journey
nodes.content: You've mastered the fundamentals! Choose your next learning direction.

nodes.title: Course Complete!
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've completed Cybersecurity Basics and learned essential concepts including password security, phishing attack recognition, and social engineering defense. Strong passwords and threat awareness are critical foundations for protecting yourself and your organization. Continue your journey by exploring advanced topics like multi-factor authentication, network security, encryption, and incident response to build comprehensive security expertise.
